chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Systems simulation structure, logic and methodologies; development of simulation models; data handling methods; analysis of simulation data; verification and validation; system simulation languages, model and analysis; application to industrial situations.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade C or better in ISEN 230 and ISEN 310.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None.

chunks.title: Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
(1) develop models using commercially available discrete event (process-oriented) simulation software,
(2) interpret simulation output using valid statistical methods,
(3) collect and analyze input data using valid statistical methods, and
(4) apply simulation to model industrial and system engineering problems.
ABET outcome: 6. An ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze and interpret data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Textbook: Jerry Banks, John S. Carson II, Barry L. Nelson, and David M. Nicol (2010). Discrete-Event Simulation (Fifth Edition), Pearson-Prentice Hall.
References: Averill M. Law (2015). Simulation Modeling & Analysis (5th Edition), McGraw-Hill.
Jeffrey A. Joines and Stephen D. Roberts (2012). Simulation Modeling with SIMIO: A Workbook (http://www.simio.com/publications/SMSWorkbook/).
Jeff Smith (Industrial and Systems Engineering, Auburn University) found at the web site https://jsmith.co/educational-modules/learning-simio-lab-series-Isls/ (labs will be partially based on these videos).

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Homework, quizzes and labs count for 14% of the grade. Each lecture exam counts for 14.25%, each lab exam counts for 18%, and the final counts for 21.5% of the grade; however, if you choose not to take the final, each lecture exams will count for 19% and each lab exam will count for 24% of the grade. Grades assigned are A for 90%-100%, B for 80%—89.9%, C for 70%-79.9%, D for 60%-69.9% and F for less than 60%. Any possibility of a curve on grades will be announced after the last midterm exam and before the final. Grades will be maintained online at http://canvas.tamu.edu
At any time, without warning, a short quiz based on previous lecture, homework or in-class examples might be given. Quizzes and homework are 3 to 5 points each and labs are 10 points, although some of the more complex lab assignments may be worth more than 10 points.
Homework will not be collected, but it will be graded through canvas using off-class quizzes that have to be completed by specified deadlines. Off-class quizzes can be re-taken several times before the specified deadline so that the homework can be fully understood. The grade corresponding to the last quiz taken will be assigned to the homework.
Most lab assignments will be due and submitted by the end of the day on the day following the day of the lab. Lab assignments are submitted through canvas. Late assignments are not accepted; therefore, you should always plan on submitting assignments early in case the computer system goes down. Two cautions with respect to canvas: (1) multiple submissions are allowed and only the latest will be graded, and (2) it is your responsibility to make sure that the file(s) you are submitting get into canvas.
Lab assignments and homework often involve turning in output from a simulation run and the actual simio model, but the actual program is only checked as needed; therefore, you can easily obtain a perfect score on homework and lab assignments with an incorrect program. You must check your program against the program solution posted on canvas to ensure that you understand the Simio concepts correctly. (This is not true for tests; I will check your programs developed during tests as thoroughly as possible.)
On occasion and without notice, attendance will be taken as a canvas quiz. These attendance quizzes will be factored in the portion of the grade corresponding to the homework, quizzes, and lab.

chunks.title: Use of Artificial Intelligence Policy
chunks.content: The use of artificial intelligence (AI) on tests and for lab assignments is prohibited. Using Al with the purpose of getting answers to homework is also prohibited. Although not recommended, the use of Al for homework is acceptable for understanding and learning the material

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Lab and homework assignments are not accepted after their deadline is passed. Usually, lab assignments must be turned in before midnight on the day after the day of the lab assignment was given, and canvas will not permit submission after the deadline.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy. Since late work may not be accepted by canvas, students should submit late work due to an excused absence to me by email (use your TAMU email account) together with documentation regarding the excused absence.
Late lab submissions may be accepted at the discretion of the instructor and assessed a penalty commensurate with the reasons and the tardiness.

chunks.title: Makeup Exam Policy
chunks.content:
Makeup exams will be considered for university valid excuses as described below in the Makeup Work Policy.
Makeup exams will be given on Apr 22 at ETB1027 at: 2:20 PM (tentatively) and will include all the material covered in the semester. The grade in the makeup exam will be the grade assigned to the missed exam. At the discretion of the instructor, under special circumstances, and upon student request, makeup exams may be given at some other time.

chunks.title: Required Software
chunks.content: The Industrial and Systems Engineering Department uses Simio, a fourth generation software system, under a grant from Simio LLC (https://www.simio.com). While in the classroom, I suggest that you use the simio installed in the classroom computers. For studying outside the classroom, I recommend that you use the free "Student At-Home Use License" to run simio in the cloud so that you can easily practice simulation modeling at home. To get the simio license you must be registered in the course and fill an application found at the Simio website. When filling out the application, you must supply a Student License Claim Code that you can find in Canvas under the item GetSimio in the module "Student Resources". To use this cloud-based simio license it is essential that you have a reliable internet connection. Alternatively, you can also use simio for free through the virtual desktop of the ISEN cloud using the VMware Horizon Client (see https://vdi.engr.tamu.edu/ for help with the Horizon Client.) Simio is not compatible with the Mac OS, hopefully the free cloud-based simio works with a Mac. If it does not work, Mac users can use the VMware Horizon Client and access simio through the virtual desktop.

chunks.title: Asking Questions
chunks.content: Please feel free to ask questions during class or after class. During class you may raise your hand to call my attention. Because of the classroom size I may not be able to hear the question and may ask students closer to the front to relay your question to me. Questions after class or in office hours, in person or by e-mail are always welcome.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
| Week | Lecture Topic | Laboratory | |---|---|---| | 1 (1/12) | Introduction to the course, introduction to simulation, and relationship with probability and statistics | Simio User Interface: Driver's License Office | | 2 (1/19) | Output analysis, confidence intervals and probability distributions | Simio help: Branching, time in the system | | 3 (1/25) | Non-stationary arrival process introduction | Model entity state variables, model entity properties, feedback loops | | 4 (2/02) | Input analysis and goodness of fit tests | Work in progress, capacity, plots and pie charts | | 5 (2/09) | Distribution fitting and parameter estimation using statistical methods | Model Entity State Variables and Properties: Node List: Tally Stat. | | 6 (2/16) | Random number generation | Animation in simio | | 7 (2/23) | Random Variate Generation
Exam 1 (Lecture) – Tentatively Feb 23 | Exam 2 (Lab) – Tentatively Feb 27 | | 8 (3/02) | Statistical analysis, comparing systems | Model state variables, Tally, Output, State statistics | | 9 (3/09) | Spring Break – No Classes | Spring Break – No Lab | | 10 (3/16) | Non-stationary arrival processes | Simio Processes Including Stations | | 11 (3/23) | Introduce Sequences/Routing | Sequences | | 12 (3/30) | Simio and Excel | No Lab | | 13 (4/06) | Transporters | Combiners and Separators | | 14 (4/13) | Verification and validation
Exam 3 (Lecture) - Tentatively Apr 15 | Cost and profit | | 15 (4/20) | Optional Simulation Topics | Exam 4 (Lab) – Tentatively Apr 24 | | 16 (4/27) | Semester Wrap-up | Arrival Tables, Resources | *Time durations for the topics are tentative and depend on class response 355-502 - Final Exam (optional) – Monday May 4 @ 8:00 AM – 10:00 AM 355-503 - Final Exam (optional) – Tuesday May 5 @ 10:30 AM – 12:30 PM

chunks.title: Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.
As per university policy, class attendance is not optional. You are expected to attend physically all class lectures and labs scheduled within the official university schedule, except for university excused absences. Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
With an excused absence, it is still the student's responsibility to find out about homework assignment and be ready for a quiz. Because we often have class or lab quizzes or computer work at any time, it is also important that you plan on attending the lecture from the start to the end of the class period. If you have an excused absence or want to review lectures, you may see most lecture notes on canvas.
Also, please note that the rule regarding job interviews is as follows: "Mandatory interviews for permanent, full-time employment or full-time internships (including those that are part of a cooperative education program) that have a duration of at least 10 weeks, provided that such interviews are related to the student's academic program and provided that the interviews are fixed date by employer policy and cannot be rescheduled. A student may not request excused absences for employment or internship interviews for more than one scheduled class meeting in one academic term.” Also, “Documentation from the employer must be provided at least 5 business days in advance."

chunks.title: Student Observances for Religious Holy Days
chunks.content: In accordance with Texas Education Code §51.911(b) and Texas A&M Student Rule 7: Attendance, students shall be excused from attending classes or other required activities, including examinations, for the observance of a religious holy day, including travel for that purpose. For more information about excused absences due to religious holy days, please visit the Faculty Affairs website (under Other University Guidelines).

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor. Medical excuses are to be provided within two business days of the medical condition. Retroactive medical excuses (a medical excuse for a condition that occurred in the past) will not be considered for an excused absence.
Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do."
"Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case" (Section 20.1.2.3, Student Rule 20).
You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures, academic integrity, and your rights and responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.
It is acceptable for you to discuss both lab assignments and homework with colleagues; it is also permissible to show another student your code (except during tests or quizzes) as long as you DO NOT COPY or USE each other's work. After discussing an assignment with someone else, do not write anything that you do not understand and always use your own language on the assignments. Obviously, you may not discuss exams with anyone else until after the exam is over and you know that every student you are discussing the exam with has already taken it.
No leniency is given for academic dishonesty during a test. Any collaboration during a test is obviously considered cheating. Use of cell phones during exams or quizzes will be considered an act of academic dishonesty and sanctions will be applied accordingly. In addition, opening any file during a lab test except were explicitly authorized is also considered cheating. A report will be made to the Aggie Honor Office regarding cheating during a test with a recommended course grade of F*.

chunks.title: Plagiarism Policy
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. Al text generators (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to:
* Creating or revising drafts
* Editing your work
* Reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.
Such use of Al text generators in this manner could be considered plagiarism and cheating according to Student Rule 20. More information may also be found at https://aggiehonor.tamu.edu or you may contact your instructor if you have questions.

chunks.title: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing equitable access to learning opportunities for all students. If you experience barriers to your education due to a disability or think you may have a disability, please contact the Disability Resources office on your campus (resources listed below) Disabilities may include, but are not limited to attentional, learning, mental health, sensory, physical, or chronic health conditions. All students are encouraged to discuss their disability related needs with Disability Resources and their instructors as soon as possible.
Disability Resources is located in the Student Services Building or at (979) 845-1637 or visit disability.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Title IX and Statement on Limits to Confidentiality
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to fostering a learning environment that is safe and productive for all. University policies and federal and state laws prohibit gender-based discrimination and sexual harassment, including sexual assault, sexual exploitation, domestic violence, dating violence, and stalking.
With the exception of some medical and mental health providers, all university employees (including full and part-time faculty, staff, paid graduate assistants, student workers, etc.) are Mandatory Reporters and must report to the Title IX Office if the employee experiences, observes, or becomes aware of an incident that meets the following conditions (see University Rule 08.01.01.M1):
* The incident is reasonably believed to be discrimination or harassment.
* The incident is alleged to have been committed by or against a person who, at the time of the incident, was (1) a student enrolled at the University or (2) an employee of the University.
Mandatory Reporters must file a report regardless of how the information comes to their attention – including but not limited to face-to-face conversations, a written class assignment or paper, class discussion, email, text, or social media post. Although Mandatory Reporters must file a report, in most instances, a person who is subjected to the alleged conduct will be able to control how the report is handled, including whether or not to pursue a formal investigation. The University's goal is to make sure you are aware of the range of options available to you and to ensure access to the resources you need.
Students wishing to discuss concerns related to mental and/or physical health in a confidential setting are encouraged to make an appointment with University Health Services or download the TELUS Health Student Support app for 24/7 access to professional counseling in multiple languages. Walk-in services for urgent, non-emergency needs are available during normal business hours at University Health Services locations; call 979.458.4584 for details.
Students can learn more about filing a report, accessing supportive resources, and navigating the Title IX investigation and resolution process on the University's Title IX webpage.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content: Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.

chunks.title: Pregnancy Accommodations
chunks.content: Texas A&M provides reasonable accommodations to students due to pregnancy and/or related conditions, such as childbirth, recovery and lactation. Students should contact the University's Pregnancy Coordinator as soon as they become aware of the need for accommodation. Depending on the circumstances, accommodations could include extended time to complete assignments or exams, changes in course sequence, or modifications to the physical classroom environment. Texas A&M will also allow a voluntary leave of absence, ensure the availability of lactation space, and maintain grievance procedures to provide for the prompt and equitable resolution of complaints of sex discrimination. For information regarding pregnancy accommodations, email TIX.Pregnancy@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Course Materials and Copyright Issues
chunks.content: All documents used in this course are copyrighted. Here, "documents" means all materials generated for this class, including, but not limited to, syllabi, quizzes, exams, problem sets, and all materials appearing on the class web site. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy any of them for any purpose other than your own personal academic use unless I expressly grant permission. Course materials may not be given or sold to any for-profit enterprise.
